[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[court dress]] worn by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[Zhouli]]'' and ''[[Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]] on (CatAlot)
revision.text:
[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[court dress]] worn by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[Zhouli]]'' and ''[[Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Kōrozen no hō|ja|黄櫨染御袍}} * {{ill|Ōtan no hō|ja|黄丹袍}} * {{ill|Forbidden colors|ja|禁色}}
== External Links == * [[Costume Museum]]: "[http://www.iz2.or.jp/fukushoku/f_disp.php?page_no=0000041 Winter Clothing of Chamberlains with Kikujin no hō]."

[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[court dress]] worn by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[Zhouli]]'' and ''[[Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Kōrozen no hō|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōtan no hō|ja|黄丹袍|12=draft}} * {{ill|Forbidden colors|ja|禁色|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [[Costume Museum]]: "[http://www.iz2.or.jp/fukushoku/f_disp.php?page_no=0000041 Winter Clothing of Chamberlains with Kikujin no hō]."

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[court dress]] worn by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[Zhouli]]'' and ''[[Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Kōrozen no hō|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ōtan no hō|ja|黄丹袍|12=draft}} * {{ill|Forbidden colors|ja|禁色|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [[Costume Museum]]: "[http://www.iz2.or.jp/fukushoku/f_disp.php?page_no=0000041 Winter Clothing of Chamberlains with Kikujin no hō]." <!--Kikujin robe-->

[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[court dress]] worn by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. 45 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:麹塵袍 up to rev 74683619
revision.text:
[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|麹塵袍を着た[[後二条天皇|後二条院]]の肖像（『[[天子摂関御影]]』、14世紀前半以前）]] '''麴塵袍'''（きくじんのほう）は、[[天皇]]が臨時祭の庭座、賭弓、弓場始など小儀の際に着用する[[束帯]]装束の、[[麹塵]]色の[[袍]]のことである。'''青色袍'''（あおいろのほう）、'''青白橡袍'''（あおしらつるばみのほう）等とも言う。
「[[麹塵]]」は[[コウジカビ]]の菌糸の色と言われ、古くは『[[周礼]]』の王后の六服の一つ「鞠衣」の古注に、これは「黄桑服で、色は鞠塵（麹塵）のようであり、桑の葉の生えはじめを象っている」とあり、中国においては黄色系の色であったと言われる<ref>小嶋汀「和様の成立: 特に青色について」関東学院女子短期大学『短大論叢』28巻、1966年。『[[釈名]]』では、鞠衣は[[菊花]]の色のような黄色とする。また『[[白氏文集]]』に麹塵の用例が多く、特に春の柳の花の色とされる。</ref>。日本では[[平安時代]]の[[9世紀]]末から用例が見られるが、遅くとも[[10世紀]]半ばの『[[西宮記]]』の頃には、「'''青白橡'''」（あおしろつるばみ）と同色とみなされ、青（緑）系の色であった。麹塵の色を単に「'''青色'''」とも言い、後には[[ヤマバト]]の色と近いことから、「'''山鳩色'''」とも呼ばれた。
== 着用例 == [[平安時代]]前期以降、[[天皇]]・[[皇太子]]・[[太上天皇|上皇]]・臣下の着用例が見られた。特に正月内宴・野[[行幸]]では天皇が'''赤白橡'''、臣下が'''青白橡'''を着用する例が見られ、[[踏歌節会|男踏歌]]の袍に着用されるなど、晴の儀式において[[位階]]にかかわりなく着用したものと思われる。[[平安時代]]後期に入るとこれらの儀式も衰退し、臣下がそろって着用するということも無くなった。
=== 天皇の着用例 === [[平安時代]]後期以降の例によると、天皇は[[賀茂神社|賀茂]]・[[石清水八幡宮|石清水]][[臨時祭]]次度出御など限られた機会に着用している。 [[室町時代]]初期の書によると、文様は[[黄櫨染御袍]]と同様の桐竹鳳凰文であり、文様を織ってから後染めした。 臨時祭が中絶した[[室町時代]]後期に断絶したが、[[江戸時代]]の臨時祭復興に際し再興された。 これ以降、文様は黄櫨染御袍とまったく同形同大の、経（縦糸）緑緯（ぬき糸）黄の先染めの固織物を使用、一般の袍が近世中期以後生地の裏面を表に使うのに対し、青色御袍では表面を表に用いる。裏地は[[山科家]]が黄平絹を、[[高倉家]]が蘇芳平絹をそれぞれ用いた(『旧儀御服記』宮内庁書陵部所蔵)。夏も紗を用いず、冬と同様の固織物とし、単に仕立てた。
=== 皇太子の着用例 === 皇太子は[[読書始]]に着用し、近世の例では[[黄丹袍]]と同様の鴛鴦丸文の経緑緯黄の浮織物とした。 近世の早い時期において、後述の[[蔵人]]の麹塵袍と同様の牡丹唐草に尾長鳥文浮織の生地の袍に共裂の帯を、天皇・東宮が着用していることが遺品から判っている。
=== 上皇の着用例 === 上皇は[[平安時代]]後期から[[鎌倉時代]]に、天皇の[[行幸]]を迎える時などに着用した。 近世では菊唐草の経緑緯黄の生地が用いられ、'''赤色袍'''や橡（つるばみ－黒）袍とともに調進例がある。
=== 臣下の着用例 === 臣下の着用例としては、[[六位蔵人]]の使用が代表的なものである。 中世以降、牡丹唐草に尾長鳥の文様の浮織物が用いられた。 平安時代後期には[[束帯]]・布袴・[[衣冠]]ともに着用したが(『[[侍中群要]]』ほか)、近世ではもっぱら束帯に着用した。 近世には六位蔵人の布袴や衣冠の着用自体がまれになり束帯での出仕が普通になったからである。 中世には、4人の六位蔵人のうち、行幸では3人まで着用できるなどの慣例があったが(『装束雑事抄』)、これも近世では極臈（六位蔵人の首席）一人のみが着用した。 なお、天皇・上皇が麹塵袍を着用している時は、蔵人は遠慮して麹塵袍を着用しなかった。 また、近世では慶安の朝覲行幸では大臣の着用例がある。
[[近代]]に至り、臨時祭の廃止により天皇が着用しなくなり、六位蔵人の制もなくなったため、現在では着用されていない。
==脚注== {{Reflist}}
==参考文献 == *津田大輔「『西宮記』女装束条について: 女装束条における摺衣と青色」『古代文化研究』第17号、島根県教育庁古代文化センター、2009年
== 関連項目 == * [[黄櫨染御袍]] * [[黄丹袍]] * [[禁色]]
== 外部リンク == * [[風俗博物館]] 日本服飾史 資料「[http://www.iz2.or.jp/fukushoku/f_disp.php?page_no=0000041 蔵人麹塵袍の冬の衣冠]」
{{DEFAULTSORT:きくしんのほう}} [[Category:宮廷・公家装束]]

[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[court dress]] worn by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[Zhouli]]'' and ''[[Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].

After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.

During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

revision.text:
[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[court dress]] worn by the [[Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[Zhouli]]'' and ''[[Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

[[sn:Kikujin robe]] [[ja:麹塵袍]]
<!--Kikujin robe--><!--Kikujin robe--><!--Kikujin robe--><!--Kikujin robe--><!--Kikujin robe--><!--Kikujin robe--><!--Kikujin robe--> [[Category:Traditional clothing of the Heian period]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[:en:court dress|court dress]] worn by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[Zhouli]]'' and ''[[Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Kōrozen no hō|ja|黄櫨染御袍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen no hō|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōtan no hō|ja|黄丹袍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtan no hō|qq=}} * [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden colors]]

However, by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].

=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11677412]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11677412]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[:en:court dress|court dress]] worn by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[:en:Zhouli|Zhouli]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiming|Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[:en:modern era|modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677412]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677412]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[:en:court dress|court dress]] worn by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[:en:Zhouli|Zhouli]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiming|Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[:en:modern era|modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Kōrozen no hō|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen no hō|1=Kōrozen no gohō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11677782}} * {{ill|Ōtan no hō|ja|黄丹袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtan no hō|1=Ōni robe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11677718}} * [[:en:Forbidden colors (Japan)|Forbidden colors]]
== External Links == * [[:en:Costume Museum|Costume Museum]]: "[http://www.iz2.or.jp/fukushoku/f_disp.php?page_no=0000041 Winter Clothing of Chamberlains with Kikujin no hō]."
{{translated page|ja|麹塵袍|version=74683619|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 2]]
}}

During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[:en:modern era|modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677412]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677412]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[:en:court dress|court dress]] worn by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[:en:Zhouli|Zhouli]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiming|Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[:en:modern era|modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[:en:modern era|modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677412]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11677412]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a [[:en:court dress|court dress]] worn by the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[:en:Zhouli|Zhouli]]'' and ''[[:en:Shiming|Shiming]],'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the [[:en:modern era|modern era]], the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the {{ill|modern era|en|modern era|WD=Q3281534|lt=modern era}}, the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]]
[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a {{ill|court dress|en|court dress|WD=Q1351030|lt=court dress}} worn by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor}} during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[:en:Zhouli|Zhouli]]'' and ''{{ill|Shiming|en|Shiming|WD=Q5927580|lt=Shiming}},'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the {{ill|modern era|en|modern era|WD=Q3281534|lt=modern era}}, the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Terms == * {{ill|Kōrozen no hō|ja|黄櫨染御袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrozen no hō|1=Kōrozen no gohō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11677782}} * {{ill|Ōtan no hō|ja|黄丹袍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtan no hō|1=Ōni robe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11677718}} * {{ill|Forbidden colors (Japan)|en|Forbidden colors (Japan)|WD=Q11591157|lt=Forbidden colors}}

=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the {{ill|modern era|en|modern era|WD=Q3281534|lt=modern era|1=Modern era}}, the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:麹塵袍]]
[[File:Emperor Go-Nijō.jpg|thumb|Portrait of Emperor Go-Nijō wearing a ''Kikujin no hō'' (麹塵袍), from the ''Tenshi Sekkan Myōei,'' early 14th century.]] '''Kikujin no hō''' (麹塵袍) is a {{ill|court dress|en|court dress|qid=Q1351030|lt=court dress|1=Court dress}} worn by the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor of Japan}} during minor ceremonies such as temporary festivals, archery rituals, and initiation ceremonies. This ''hō'' (robe) is colored in a ''Kikujin'' hue, also referred to as ''Aoiro no hō'' (青色袍) or ''Aoshiratsurubami no hō'' (青白橡袍).
"Kikujin" refers to a color said to resemble the mycelium of [[aspergillus mold]]. Historical Chinese texts, such as the annotations on ''[[:en:Zhouli|Zhouli]]'' and ''{{ill|Shiming|en|Shiming|qid=Q5927580|lt=Shiming|1=Shiming}},'' associate this color with a yellowish hue resembling early mulberry leaves or chrysanthemum blossoms. However, by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, ''Kikujin'' had become associated with a blue-green hue in Japan, equivalent to ''Aoshiratsurubami'' or "light blue oak." It was also referred to simply as "blue" or ''yamabato-iro'' (山鳩色), likened to the plumage of [[mountain pigeons]].
== Examples of Wear == === By the Emperor === From the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the Emperor wore the ''Kikujin no hō'' during limited occasions, such as [[temporary festivals]] at [[Kamo shrines|Kamo Shrine]] or [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]]. During the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the robe's design featured motifs similar to the [[Kōrozen no hō]] (黄櫨染御袍), including paulownia, bamboo, and phoenix patterns.
After the interruption of temporary festivals in the late {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the custom ceased. However, the robe was restored during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} when these festivals were revived. The material used was a solid weave of green warp and yellow weft threads. Unlike other robes that displayed the fabric's underside, the ''Kikujin no hō'' was worn with the front side of the fabric outward.
=== By the Crown Prince === The Crown Prince donned the ''Kikujin no hō'' during the [[Dokushoshime]] (Reading Ceremony). Early examples included robes with motifs of ''oshidori maru'' (mandarin ducks) and other intricate patterns.
=== By the Retired Emperor === The retired Emperor wore the robe for occasions such as greeting the reigning Emperor's visits. Patterns included chrysanthemums on green warp and yellow weft fabrics.
=== By Court Officials === Officials, especially the ''[[roku-i kurōdo]]'' (Sixth-Rank Chamberlains), were known for wearing the ''Kikujin no hō.'' In the [[:en:Medieval period|Medieval period]], they used robes with peony arabesques and long-tailed bird motifs.
During formal occasions, they paired the robe with ''bofuku'' or ''ikkan,'' a practice that dwindled in later periods. By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, only the chief chamberlain wore this attire during special rituals.
== Decline == With the abolition of temporary festivals and the chamberlain system in the {{ill|modern era|en|modern era|qid=Q3281534|lt=modern era|1=Modern era}}, the ''Kikujin no hō'' is no longer worn.
